title: Listening - Part 4: Learning a Foreign Language
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people discussing their experiences and motivations for learning a new language in adulthood

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d always been a bit of a homebody, but [21]I decided to learn Japanese primarily because I’d been offered a lucrative three-year contract in Tokyo[/21] and I didn't want to rely on translators. I wanted to be independent. [26]The most rewarding aspect has been the profound insight into the local culture that only language can provide[/26]. I realized that so many social nuances are tied to specific verbs and honorifics. It’s made my transition to life in Japan much smoother and more respectful. || Speaker 2: I’m a retired history professor, and [22]I took up Italian because I wanted to be able to read original Renaissance documents without needing a translation[/22]. I wanted to get closer to the primary sources. It’s been an intellectual joy. However, [27]I found the sheer speed of native speakers in Rome to be incredibly intimidating[/27] during my first visit. I could read Dante, but I couldn't order a coffee without panicking! I’ve had to focus much more on my listening skills lately, which has been a humbling but necessary shift in my studies. || Speaker 3: I recently discovered that my great-grandparents were originally from Poland, and [23]I started learning the language as a way to honor my heritage and connect with my roots[/23]. It’s been a very emotional journey. [28]What I didn't expect was the massive boost in my overall memory and cognitive flexibility[/28]. Since I started, I’ve found I’m much better at multitasking and problem-solving in my daily life. It’s like my brain has been given a thorough workout every single day, and I feel much sharper at fifty than I did at forty. || Speaker 4: I’m a volunteer at a local refugee center, and [24]I decided to learn Arabic because I wanted to be able to offer more direct support and comfort to the families arriving in my town[/24]. I wanted to break down the barriers of communication. [29]The biggest hurdle has been the complex script and the fact that there are so many different regional dialects[/29]. It’s been a very slow process, and there are days when I feel I’m making no progress at all. But when I see the look of relief on someone’s face when I say a few words in their own tongue, it makes every hour of study worth it. || Speaker 5: I’d always felt like a bit of a tourist whenever I traveled, so [25]I started learning French because I wanted to experience the world in a more authentic way[/25]. I wanted to stay in small villages and actually talk to the locals. [30]The most unexpected benefit has been the wonderful new social circle I’ve found at my local evening class[/30]. We’re a diverse group of people who never would have met otherwise. We now go out for dinner once a month and only speak French, which has turned a solitary academic pursuit into a vibrant and supportive community effort.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what primarily motivated each speaker to learn the language.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 learned Japanese because of a job contract in Tokyo.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted to read original historical documents in Italian.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to honor his Polish heritage and connect with his roots.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted to offer direct support to refugees in her town.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted a more authentic travel experience by talking to locals.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions that the language provided profound insight into the local culture.

options.A: to facilitate a move to a new country
options.C: to meet a requirement of their employment
options.D: to prepare for a specific academic exam
options.F: to enhance their experience of foreign travel
options.G: to access literature in its original form
options.H: to assist a vulnerable group in society

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as an outcome or difficulty.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 highlights the cultural insights gained through the language.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 found the speed of native speakers to be intimidating.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 mentions a boost in memory and cognitive flexibility.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 mentions the complexity of the script and dialects as a hurdle.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 highlights the new social circle she found in her evening class.

options.A: a realization that they have a talent for linguistics
options.B: the development of a new social network
options.C: the discovery of a lucrative new career
options.D: an improvement in their general mental capacity
options.E: a feeling of frustration with the grammar
options.F: the challenge of mastering a new writing system
options.G: struggling with the pace of natural conversation
options.H: a deeper understanding of societal customs
